NETHERLANDS. Utrecht. Harpe d'Or David (Gulden), ND (ca. 1457). Rhenen Mint. David of Burgundy. NGC AU-58.
Fr-191; Delm-941 (R2). Weight: 3.41 gms. Obverse: Bust of King David with harp seated on gothic throne above quartered arms of Burgundy and Utrecht; Reverse: Floriated cross with letters D A V I D in center and inner angles. The sole finest example certified of this stunning medieval RARITY depicting the biblical King David of ancient Israel. This portrayal, especially when placed directly above the Burgundy coat of arms, invites a comparison between the ancient David and his namesake David of Burgundy, suggesting in the latter the characteristics of strength, wisdom, and humility often associated with the former. Well-struck and gently lustrous, with only a trace of wear present and pleasing ochre patination throughout.
Finest Certified Medieval Gulden Illustrating King David with Harp

sales.title:
SWEDEN. Coup d'État, Restrike Silver Medal, 1772. Gustav III. NGC MS-64.
Hild-19; Delzanno-26a; Bruun-17896. Diameter: 57 mm. Weight: 80 gms. By Carl Gustaf Fehrman (C.G. FEHRMAN / C. FEHRMAN). Mirror-like surfaces and a spectacular, high relief in the king's portrait. Very rare in this high grade.

Gustav III was supposed to be merely a figurehead, like his father, Adolf Frederik, but only a year after his coronation he instigated a coup d'état with the help of the military after which the powers were divided between the Riksdag and the king. On the reverse Nike, the goddess of victory, advances holding an oak wreath and palm leaves, an ancient symbols of triumph.


From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

sales.title:
1788 Massachusetts Cent. Ryder 13-N, W-6380. Rarity-4+. No Period After MASSACHUSETTS. EF-40 (PCGS).
Pleasing autumn-brown patina, both sides also exhibit subtle steel-olive outlines to many of the design elements. There is some isolated roughness in the planchet, largely as made, the only significant blemishes an ancient carbon deposit in the upper left obverse field and light encrustation in the reverse field at upper left and lower right. Well centered in strike with overall detail bold apart from minor softness in the centers, where much of the wear is concentrated. The Ryder 13-N is the scarcer of just two die varieties that comprise the overall No Period Guide Book variety of the 1788 Massachusetts cent. PCGS EF-40 is a commendable grade for this Ryder number as it is rare any finer.

sales.title: IONIA. Phocaea. Ca. 387-326 BC. EL hecte (10mm, 2.46 gm). NGC VF 2/5 - 2/5, flan flaws. Female head left / Irregular quadripartite incuse square. Bodenstedt -. Cf. Bodenstedt 108 for similar type.   Situated on the western coast of modern-day Turkey, Phocaea was an ancient Ionian city celebrated for its maritime prowess and commercial acumen. This mercantile dynamism was reflected in the city's distinct contribution to the history of coinage. Phocaea was among the earliest cities to produce electrum coinage, a naturally occurring alloy of gold and silver that was a precursor to the later, more standardized coinage in pure gold and silver. This Phocaean electrum coinage, minted around the sixth through fourth centuries BC, was characterized by a range of richly symbolic designs, including mystical creatures, prominent deities, and civic emblems, all indicative of their vibrant cultural life and marked by exceptional detail and high relief owing to the skill of Ionian die engravers. Salzburg
Max Gandolph von Küenburg, 1668-1687.
10 Ducats 1686, Salzburg. SS: RVDBERTVS · ET · VIRGILIVS · PATRONI · SALISBVRGENS: Saint Rupert and Saint Virgil facing each other and holding long crosiers / MAX: GAND: S: R: E: CARD. ARCHIEP: ETPR: SAL: S: SED: AP: LE: Coat of arms surmounted by the archiepiscopal hat. 34,76g. Fr. 805; Probszt -.
NGC AU58 (Top pop).
(2117153-015).
The only specimen known. Good extremely fine.
Hess-Divo 305 (25 October 2006), 391.
By modesty or tradition, many Salzburg coins do not feature a portrait of the archbishop, but only his coat of arms. Instead of the expected portrait, we find the depiction of Salzburg's two patron saints, St. Rupert and St. Virgil. Rupert was of high nobility, perhaps even of royal descent. In 696, he received the remains of a ruined ancient city, Juvavum, from the Duke of Bavaria. He built a church there, as well as several monasteries. He also developed the salt mines, which led to a change in the town's name to Salzburg.

1787 Fugio Cent. Pointed Rays. Newman 12-S, W-6805. Rarity-5-. STATES UNITED, 4 Cinquefoils. VF-35 (PCGS).
160.8 grains. This light golden-brown, overall smooth-looking example is struck on a nice planchet and remains free of troublesome handling marks. The only flan flaw present is minor and out of the way at the lower reverse border, just before 6 o'clock; some ancient surface build up within the letters of WE ARE ONE and an associated spot above the label are the only mentionable post-production blemishes. Obverse slightly aligned to 12 o'clock, upper border engaging the top of the sun, reverse trivially aligned to 7 o'clock with broader border at upper right. Detail is universally bold and pleasing at the certified Choice VF level. While a few sharper examples of this Newman number have passed through our auctions in recent years, including the primary Richard August coin in PCGS EF-45, that offered here is still an above average example of the variety that will fit comfortably into many collection.
From the Richard August Collection.

sales.title:
1652 Oak Tree Threepence. Noe-28, Salmon 6-B, W-310. Rarity-4. No IN on Obverse. AU-55 (PCGS).
15.3 grains. A glorious example that belongs in any discussion of the finest Noe-28 Oak Trees in numismatic hands. Rich pewter, mauve and steel gray shades are present on one or both sides, the reverse a bit more deeply toned overall than the obverse. Centering is good, the peripheral legends fully legible even though a shallow straight clip at upper right relative to the obverse involves the tops of the letters HV on that side, EN in ENGLAND on the reverse. The tree is sharp throughout its branches and roots, date and denomination both equally crisp. There is some light, ancient surface build up engaging certain peripheral letters, but no troublesome marks are seen, and the in hand appearance is impressively smooth. Frosty in texture with plenty of good gloss, this coin would ideally represent either the type or variety in an advanced collection.

Surprising given its modest Rarity-4 rating, the last Noe-28 Oak Tree we offered was the PCGS VF-30 Kendall coin in our March 2015 Baltimore Auction; the extensive Sydney F. Martin Collection lacked this Noe number. The Hain-Partrick coin (Noe-28.5) in NGC MS-63 and the Roper-Salmon specimen in NGC MS-62 are among the very finest, followed closely by the Richard August coin offered here. All three are head and shoulders above the Wurzbach-Ford coin, certified EF-40 by PCGS as of our November 2012 Baltimore Auction offering, which serves as both the Noe and Salmon plate, and nor is the Salmon Noe-28.5 in NGC EF-45 anywhere near as nice. The difficulty that collectors will experience in locating a truly superb Noe-28 Oak Tree is not widely appreciated, and the astute bidder would be wise to take full advantage of this important offering among the Richard Auction Massachusetts silver.
From the Richard August Collection. Earlier ex Romano.
PCGS# 929511. BASE PCGS# 18. NGC ID: 2ARF.

1796 Draped Bust Cent. S-116, B-36. Rarity-5-. Reverse of 1795. Fine-15 (PCGS).
Breen Die State II. Deep steely charcoal-copper patina with glints of warm rose-brown color evident here and there around the centers. Nice and glossy, the surfaces reveal some ancient build up in the protected areas around and within some of the design elements, but there are no marks of note apart from a few dull nicks after the second letter S in STATES. Minor peripheral softness involves the tops of the letters TY in LIBERTY on the obverse and ES OF AMERICA on the reverse, but otherwise this coin is well struck and retains plenty of bold detail in the presence of moderately heavy wear. The PCGS Fine-15 grade aligns nicely with EAC standards, suggesting a ranking of tied for CC#9 for the challenging Sheldon-116 dies per the 2000 Bland census.
From the Widmer Family Collection. Earlier ex Chris McCawley, August 5, 2002; COL Steven K. Ellsworth; Heritage's sale of the COL Steven Ellsworth Collection of U.S. Large Cents 1796-1814, January 2025 FUN Signature Auction, lot 2025.

1760 Voce Populi Halfpenny. Nelson-14, Zelinka 16-O, W-13960. Rarity-6. P Below Bust. AU-58 (PCGS).
115.8 grains. A sharp and inviting piece with hard, frosty surfaces toned in dominant medium brown. There is some variegated color at lower right obverse, both darker and lighter, reverse with a fleck of ancient scale atop the seated figure's left shoulder. Shallow clips at 4 and 10 o'clock relative to the obverse are as made, there are no planchet flaws, and the overall detail is sharp despite the fact that the left reverse border encroaches upon the letters HIBE.

Just shy of Mint State standing, this coin continues the run of premium quality Voce Populi halfpennies in our current offering of the Richard August Collection. It is superior to all other Nelson-14s we can recall handling, its only rival another PCGS AU-58 (with CAC approval) that brought $4,700 in our February 2014 Americana Sale. That coin, ex Ted Craige, was described as,

...likely the finest known Nelson-14. At very least it is the finest example of this rare variety that we are aware of, being finer than the November 2004 C4 example (lot 51, VF-30 with dark, granular surfaces), the Norweb-McGrath specimen (C4 November 2007, lot 57, EF-40 and described as "at or near the top of Condition Census"), the finer of the two Taylor pieces (March 1987, lot 2041, VF-30 or better), and the Ford specimen (October 2004, lot 26, Choice EF, described as "one of the nicest seen").

The Craige coin has a couple of pin scratches on the obverse, at the shoulder, and Mr. August's particularly choice AU-58 is clearly finer.
From the Richard August Collection.
PCGS# 924120. BASE PCGS# 268. NGC ID: 2AUF.

sales.title:
1787 Fugio Cent. Pointed Rays. Newman 9-S, W-6765. Rarity-6. STATES UNITED, 4 Cinquefoils. EF-40 (PCGS).
156.5 grains. An important offering for Newman 9-S, a rare and conditionally challenged variety. This is a generally bold EF with softness confined to the upper obverse and lower reverse, involving the sun and ring at 6 o'clock, respectively. Original planchet texture remains in the former area, but despite the softness some features in the sun's face and rays are discernible. The impression is drawn to 3 o'clock on the obverse, affecting only the denticulation, reverse a bit better centered, but with scant denticulation at lower left. Color is a nice walnut-brown, seemingly secondary as there is evidence here of a light, ancient cleaning. No sizeable or otherwise significant blemishes are evident. The die state is late, Newman C/A, with the obverse boldly clashed through the date.

There are very few nice examples of this underrated variety, the nicest of which is impounded in the American Numismatic Society Collection, after being the sole Newman 9-S discovered in the Bank of New York hoard. The PCGS AU-53 that brought $5,040 in our November 2024 sale was a new addition to the census. Retz's CC4 was the Scarinci coin that ended up in the Syd Martin Collection; graded AU-50 by PCGS, it was described as displaying "deep ebony patina...with a light granularity throughout" when it sold in our August 2023 sale. The only other example graded better than VF offered since we sold the Mint State Norweb coin back in January 2002 was Mr. August's front-line example. Also certified EF-40 by PCGS, but somewhat more original in appearance than this one, it realized $3,840 in our Summer (August) 2025 Global Showcase Auction. We've offered just 10 examples from these dies since 2010, three of which were in Details grade holders. Ford lacked this variety entirely.
From the Richard August Collection.
PCGS# 878083. BASE PCGS# 883. NGC ID: 2B8E.

sales.title:
1787 Excelsior Copper. W-5785. Rarity-6. Eagle on Globe Facing Left. EF-40 (PCGS).
132.7 grains. An outstanding example of this classic New York issue from the early federal era. The surfaces are lovely and host warm olive-brown patina with hints of faded rose nestled within the protected areas around the central design elements. Most motifs are boldly to sharply defined, the overall light wear required for PCGS to return the EF-40 grade most noticeable within the shield at central obverse, although even so the sun and mountains are easily discerned. The strike is well executed, the centering quite good with the obverse border only lightly encroaching on the liberty cap and eagle's outstretched wing at upper left, upper left reverse border likewise just barely touching the tops of the letters US in PLURIBUS. There off-center impression has left a thin lip of virgin flan at lower right reverse, beyond the letters UM in UNUM and the following stars. Not even close inspection with a loupe reveals any significant planchet issues, either pre- or post-strike, and similar magnification is required to discern a few trivial nicks in the upper left reverse field at the bases of the letters BU in PLURIBUS and some ancient surface build up within the recesses of the standing figures on the obverse, eagle's plumage on the reverse. The in-hand appearance is smooth with a hard, tight appearance. The eye appeal is absolutely first rate for a lightly circulated example of this challenging type.

With the Articles of Confederation reserving coinage rights for the individual states, in early 1787 several petitions reached the Legislature in New York seeking approval to issue copper coins. In the end, however, the New York Legislature decided against authorizing a new coinage and instead opted to regulate the copper coins already in circulation. Even so, several unauthorized copper issues made their appearance in the state, primarily from the private mint known as Machin's Mills, located at the outlet of Orange Pond near Newburgh, and those enterprises operated by John Bailey and Ephraim Brasher in and near New York City. The Nova Eborac and Excelsior pieces are attributed to Bailey and Brasher, although beyond that little is known either of their overall coinage operations or the specifics of these issues.

The Excelsior pieces are far rarer than their Nova Eborac counterparts. There are three basic varieties of the former type, combining an obverse with the New York Arms and the word EXCELSIOR with a Heraldic Eagle reverse. One of those varieties shows a Heraldic Eagle with the arrows in the talon at left, the so-called Transposed Arrows reverse, W-5775. The other two use the same Heraldic Eagle dies but two different New York Arms dies, one showing the tiny eagle atop the shield facing to the left, as seen here, and the other showing that tiny eagle facing to the right (W-5780, offered above). The Transposed Arrows variety is the rarest, with just six known. This is the more common of the two others, although we caution bidders that most survivors of the W-5785 in numismatic hands are in low grades, and often with significant impairments. Indeed, our typical offering for this type is in the Fine to VF grade range, with surface and environmental damage topping the list among problems to expect. Richard August's discerning eye clearly recognized the rarity of such smooth, problem-free surfaces as seen here which, when combined with above average definition, makes for a coin that would be difficult to improve upon for the collector seeking a single Excelsior copper for type purposes, or the specialist who desires a superior example of the W-5785 type. We have offered only one sharper example in recent years, the PCGS AU-50 in our March 2017 sale of the Anthony Bettancourt Collection. Yet while that piece retained more definition than seen here, it also carried several noticeable marks, including a number of nicks at central reverse and an unfortunate scrape on the obverse through the first letter E in EXCELSIOR; both examples are struck similarly off center to upper left. We find the Richard August specimen more pleasing and overall finer, and are confident that knowledgeable bidders will agree.
From the Richard August Collection.
PCGS# 427. NGC ID: 2B3M.

DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC AU Details--Peck Marked.
Hauberg-25; Hauberg Collection-; Bruun-1796. Weight: 0.99 gms. A well-struck example of this scarce type. It has an even flan and blundered legends.

The obverse is of Byzantine style, although this exact motif is not known on any Byzantine coin. It depicts Christ seated on the throne of David (in accordance with Luke 1:32-33), but here He is shown in prayer, interceding for His believers (Hebrews 7:25).
From the L.E. Bruun Collection.

Ex:Lauritz C. Petersen Collection (Copenhagen - 1914) Lot # 384 (illustrated plate IV).

sales.title:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-1075). Lund Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC AU Details--Peck Marked.
Hauberg-28; Hauberg Collection-945; Bruun-1804. (Illustrated, plate 7). Weight: 0.99 gms. Moneyer: Atsor. This fully legible example has a clear strike with a deep toning on top of its lustrous surface; small planchet crack at 2 o'clock and light verdigris.

The obverse is of Byzantine inspiration, featuring Jesus Christ with His right hand in blessing, carrying the Gospels in His left.
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.
sales.description: Stack’s Bowers and Ponterio | ['The December 2025 World Collectors Choice Online Auction']

sales.title:
DENMARK. Penny, ND (1047-75). Lund Mint. Svend II Estridsen. NGC MS-62.
Hauberg-28; Hauberg Collection-992; Bruun-1845. Weight: 1.18 gms. Moneyer: Ulfcil. Highly detailed, near Choice and with a perfectly adorable iridescent toning.

This beautiful Byzantine type was struck in the later part of Svend's reign after he had changed his name on the coins from the Nordic "Svein" to the Latin, Magnus probably to underline his Christian faith. Interestingly, in this scarce variant an E-punch has been used lying down has been used as the first letter in Magnus instead of a regular M punch.
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

KM.20 - Fr.3 ; Or - 6,44 g - 21 mm - 6 h
NGC MS 63 (8583602-026).

ROUMANIE - ROMANIA
Charles Ier de Roumanie (1866-1914). 12 1/2 lei, 40e anniversaire du règne 1906, Bruxelles. NGC AU 58 (8524350-030).
Av. CAROL I REGE AL ROMANIEI. 1866-1906. Buste en uniforme à gauche, signature A. MICHAUX.
Rv. Aigle éployée couronnée, avec une croix dans son bec, tenant une main de justice et une épée, avec banderole PRIN STATORNICIE LA ISBINDA (La constance amène la victoire) ; au-dessous 12 1/2 LEI.
Fr.8 - KM.36 ; Or - 4,04 g - 20 mm - 12 h
NGC AU 58 (8524350-030).
D’usure régulière avec de petites marques de circulation. Patine dorée. TTB à Superbe.

ROUMANIE - ROMANIA
Charles Ier de Roumanie (1866-1914). Piéfort de 5 bani 1867, Smethwick (Watt & C°). NGC MS 63 RB (6349210-012).
Av. ROMANIA. Écu couronné et soutenu par deux lions, avec banderole NIHIL SINE DEO, sur un manteau couronné.
Rv. Couronne formée d’une branche de laurier et d’une branche de chêne, au centre : 5 BANI (date) et à l’exergue WATT & C°.
KM.- (cf. Pn5-Pn6) - Sch. & St. 006-1.4 v. ; Cuivre - 25 mm - 6 h
Top Pop : c’est le seul exemplaire gradé !
NGC MS 63 RB (6349210-012).
Frappe monnaie. Avec tranche lisse, ce piéfort ne semble par répertorié en cuivre. Conserve l’essentiel de sa couleur rouge. Superbe à Fleur de coin.
